title: Turned-Leg Farmhouse Table
summary: A slab farmhouse tabletop on purchased turned legs — skip the lathe and get a furniture-shop look with an afternoon of apron building.
description:
This is the fast route to a table that looks like it came out of a real furniture shop: buy turned legs instead of making them, and put your effort into a flat, well-glued tabletop and a square apron frame instead. It's a legitimate shortcut, not a cop-out — turned legs from a specialty supplier are consistent in a way that's genuinely hard to match on a home lathe, and they let a beginner build a leg-and-apron table with zero turning experience.

The apron is what actually carries the load and keeps the legs from racking side to side, so build and square it before you attach a single leg. Save your offcuts from the tabletop boards — this plan is designed to pair with a matching bench, and there's usually enough width left in the scraps to get you most of the way there.
timeLabel: 5–7 hrs, plus glue-up drying time for the tabletop

tools.note: For flattening and squaring the tabletop glue-up.

materials.name: Turned table legs, 28-29" dining height
materials.note: Buy from a turned-leg supplier — this is what makes the shortcut work. Pick a profile that matches your apron width.

materials.name: Pine, 1x10, 8 ft
materials.note: Tabletop boards. Save the offcuts — they're sized to build a matching bench.

materials.name: Leg mounting hardware
materials.note: Whatever the leg manufacturer specifies — usually a corner bracket or threaded insert.

cutList.note: Cut from tabletop-board offcuts, not new stock.

steps.title: Glue up the tabletop
steps.body: Edge-glue the four 1x10 boards into a single tabletop panel and clamp on a flat, level surface. Alternate the direction of any visible grain cupping board to board so the whole panel resists warping evenly, and check the surface is flat with a straightedge before you walk away — a twist that sets in the glue is much harder to fix than one you catch now. Let it dry fully before moving on.

steps.title: Build the end apron/leg assemblies
steps.body: Build each end apron piece — this is what each pair of legs will mount to. Use pocket holes and glue at every joint, and check for square as you go.

steps.title: Connect with side aprons
steps.body: Join the two end assemblies with the long side apron pieces, again with pocket screws and glue. Check the whole frame for square and for a flat sit before you go further — this apron carries the whole table's structure, and any racking here shows up as a wobble later.

steps.title: Add center supports
steps.body: Install the center support pieces to brace the long span of the apron and give the tabletop extra attachment points in the middle.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Mount the four turned legs to the apron corners using whatever hardware the leg manufacturer specifies — usually corner brackets or a threaded insert. Snug every leg evenly and check the table sits flat with no rock.

steps.title: Attach the tabletop and finish
steps.body: Center the finished apron assembly on the underside of the tabletop and screw up through the apron into the top, leaving an even overhang on all sides. Sand the whole table through the grits and finish as desired.

images.alt: Farmhouse dining table with a solid pine slab top on purchased turned legs
